title: Tokenizing Batches
concept: Tokenizing prompt-response sequences and applying padding/truncation to fit context limits.

exercises.prompt: What does a loss mask do in training?

options.text: It calculates gradients for all tokens.
It only calculates gradients for code output tokens.

exercises.explain: A loss mask is used to only calculate gradients for specific tokens, like the code output tokens, while ignoring the prompt tokens.

cards.title: What is Tokenization?
cards.body: Tokenization is the process of breaking text into smaller pieces called tokens. It helps computers understand and process the language better.

cards.title: Batch Tokenization
cards.body: For training, we group multiple prompt-response pairs into batches and transform them into tokens. This helps speed up the training process.

cards.title: Tokenization Like Address Labels
cards.body: Imagine each word in a sentence is like an address label on a package. Tokenization puts a clear label on each piece of text to help a computer know what to do with it.

cards.title: Example of Tokenizing a Prompt-Response
cards.body: For the prompt 'Hello, world!', the tokens could be ['Hello', ',', 'world', '!']. Each part is now labeled, making it easier for the computer.

cards.title: Myth: Tokenization is Complicated
cards.body: Some people think tokenization is hard, but it's simply breaking down text into chunks that make sense. It's like organizing your toys by type.

cards.title: Padding and Truncation
cards.body: Padding adds extra tokens to ensure even lengths, while truncation shortens too-long sequences. Both methods help maintain a consistent size.

left.text: Adds tokens to make shorter sequences longer.

right.text: Cuts off tokens from longer sequences.

exercises.prompt: What is the main purpose of padding in tokenization?

options.text: To make all sequences the same length.

exercises.explain: Padding is used to ensure that all token sequences in a batch are of the same length, which is essential for efficient processing.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Truncation adds tokens to shorter sequences.
exercises.explain: Truncation actually reduces the length of sequences by cutting off tokens, rather than adding them.

exercises.prompt: Padding helps to make token sequences the same ______.
exercises.sentence: Padding helps to make token sequences the same length.
exercises.explain: Padding is used to adjust the lengths of token sequences so they can be processed together in batches.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes truncation?

options.text: It ensures all sequences are too long.
It shortens sequences that are too long.
It adds padding tokens to all sequences.

exercises.explain: Truncation is the process of shortening sequences that are longer than the allowed length.

exercises.prompt: During tokenization, why is it important to use both padding and truncation?

options.text: To confuse the model with extra data.
To avoid using any tokens at all.

exercises.explain: Padding and truncation together help manage variable-length inputs by ensuring all token sequences in a batch are of consistent length, which is necessary for efficient processing.
